pathophysiology_summary: A seborrhoeic keratosis is a benign epidermal proliferation, very common with age, appearing as a well-demarcated, warty/verrucous, 'stuck-on' pigmented plaque with keratin (pseudohorn) cysts on dermoscopy and no atypical pigment network/blue-white veil/arborising vessels. Here mechanical trauma (towel-drying) partially avulsed the lesion, causing a raw oozing base and pain, with bleeding exaggerated by aspirin — a mechanical event, not spontaneous ulceration of a tumour. Discriminators: the 'stuck-on' waxy morphology and pseudohorn cysts, multiplicity and stability distinguish it from melanoma (asymmetry, colour/border irregularity, evolution, ugly-duckling); the crumbly verrucous surface without a pearly rolled border/arborising vessels argues against pigmented BCC; and truncal location in an older adult without thrombosed capillaries argues against a viral wart. Crucially, a telehealth provider cannot dermoscope, so definitive remote reassurance is inappropriate — the correct approach is haemostasis plus a routine in-person skin check to confirm benignity (and biopsy any atypical/changing lesion), avoiding both ED over-escalation and false reassurance.

differential.evidence_basis: 'Stuck-on' warty pigmented lesion, one of many increasing with age, stable until mechanical trauma.
Any bleeding pigmented lesion must be assessed for melanoma; cannot dermoscope remotely — arrange in-person skin check.
Pearly rolled border/arborising vessels — the crumbly verrucous surface argues against.
Truncal location/older age and no thrombosed capillaries argue against a common wart.

differential_progression.scoring_note: Recognise likely benign SK from morphology/stability, keep melanoma on the differential, and arrange an in-person skin check to confirm (cannot dermoscope remotely) — neither ED nor absolute reassurance.

differential.evidence_basis: Stable 'stuck-on' warty lesion, bled only after mechanical trauma; multiple similar lesions.
Lower probability given stability/morphology, but confirm with an in-person dermoscopic skin check; biopsy if any atypia.

differential.diagnosis: Traumatised seborrhoeic keratosis (confirm on in-person skin check)
differential.evidence_basis: Working assessment: haemostasis + routine in-person skin check to confirm benignity; biopsy anything atypical.

red_flags.description: Genuine melanoma features (spontaneous change/growth/darkening/bleeding before trauma, ugly-duckling)
red_flags.clinical_significance: Would raise melanoma concern; here the lesion was stable and bled only after mechanical trauma. Cannot dermoscope remotely, so confirm in person.
red_flags.if_missed_consequence: A melanoma could be mislabelled as a benign SK.

red_flags.description: Uncontrolled bleeding on aspirin, or secondary infection of the raw base
red_flags.clinical_significance: On aspirin, bleeding may be prolonged; the raw base can superinfect.

diagnostic_reasoning_pitfalls.description: Declaring the lesion definitely benign over video without a dermoscopic exam, potentially missing melanoma.
Sending a mechanically-bled benign lesion to ED for suspected 'burst melanoma'.
Not accounting for aspirin in the bleeding/haemostasis advice.

telehealth_limits_for_this_case.correct_escalation_action: Firm-pressure haemostasis and a protective dressing; arrange a routine (1-2 week) in-person skin check to confirm the benign diagnosis (and biopsy any atypical lesion); ED only if bleeding is uncontrolled.
telehealth_limits_for_this_case.premature_diagnosis_risk: Absolute remote reassurance (missing melanoma) or ED over-escalation for a benign mechanically-bled lesion.

guidelines_referenced.reference: Cancer Council Australia pigmented lesion guidance
guidelines_referenced.relevant_recommendation: Seborrhoeic keratoses are benign, but any pigmented lesion that changes, bleeds spontaneously or is atypical should be examined (dermoscopy) and biopsied if suspicious.
